speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number five, in an interview with Edith Cimmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when we left off you had just met your future husband in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met him, and we got together. And we were in Austria. I wanted-- so we came in 1945. We were married in '46. And I came-- I came to Austria, and they asked us where-- it was temporarily-- where we want to go. I wanted to go to Israel very badly, because I had, there, cousins that survived the war. And I spoke Hebrew, and I wanted to be with them, together. My husband didn't want to go to Israel. He was too very sick, and he wanted to go to United States. So I almost left him in-- in-- you know, we had a fight, and I had to give in. He had a cousin in United States that-- he thought that he still a single man. He had a daughter, that she was in her late 30's-- and he's a single man, that he's going to marry her. And so he sent us some papers to go to America. He lived in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came in-- in January the 17th. The name of the ship was We left Hamburg the 1st of January. And 17 days we were on the-- on the ship. We came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Boston. And whatever had to stay in Boston, they left. And the rest, they-- they took us-- they came-- there's a HIAS organization. They took us by train from Boston to New York. We came to New York. Everybody, relatives, came to pick up the Jewish people with their families, with their children. Nobody came to us. He changed his mind, because I wrote him a letter that he's married and has a child. It was 20 months old-- she was born in Austria in 1947, on April 18. So he didn't come to show, to-- to pick us up. And I said, like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And I started to cry, and I started to fight with my husband. I said, I told you that I want to go to Israel, and I have there some cousins, somebody. Here in America, I don't have anybody. I don't know-- we don't have any money.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are poor. We don't have anything. We don't know the language, how to speak English. And why this so?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I start to cry. And why, again, the same thing-- I should have gotten killed by-- by being in the ghetto, by Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So came to us a couple. And they started to talk to us. And they say, we are from HIAS, and don't be afraid. We're the ones that are going to take care of you. We have a building, and you're going to be under-- under the HIAS, their administration. We shouldn't be afraid. So we had no choice. So the lady gave us $6 each. She gave me $6, she gave my little girl $6, and my husband $6. So we were rich, already. We had $18. So we came to HIAS. There were a lot of people, Jewish people. We were not the only one. We were there for six weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the HIAS wanted to send us-- any city in the United States we wanted, then they're going to find a job. They're going to give us furniture. They're going to find a home, and we should settle down. He wanted just to-- in New York it was very-- that was in 1949, in January. And it was crowded. It was impossible to-- they should, you know, settle us down in New York. He didn't want to go no place, nowhere-- very stubborn, stubborn like a rock. Didn't want to listen-- he doesn't want to go no place except in New York. He wants to stay in New York. I had no choice. I couldn't fight him. He didn't give in. My husband, I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: After six weeks, I said to him, you know what? I find out that every city from Europe-- like Berezne, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], you know, one next to the other-- has organization here. And they belong to-- and has a society. And they belong to the society. You watch the baby-- I'm the one that's going to be brave. I'm going to go to the office from the HIAS, and I'll talk to them. And he said, OK. So I went down to the office, and I was brave, you know. And I was-- how old was I? 22 years old, when I came to United States. I was married. I was 19. I came to the office and I say, who is the-- the director from the office? I come from this and this city. And I say, I would like to find out-- my father is from this and this city-- I was born in Berezne. He was a [NON-ENGLISH], a different city. And maybe there's-- if they have a [NON-ENGLISH] society, a group. I'm going to go there, and I would like to find out their meeting, and I'm going to say that I'm this and this daughter. And maybe they'll find us a job or something-- an apartment, because we've been here, already, for six weeks. And in Hebrew they say, [NON-ENGLISH], how long we're going to stay in this HIAS building, and stay in the line for some food. The minute I said, what? Your-- your father's Israel Kukes? I say, yes. He's my father. And he-- oh, my gosh! That's your father? Are you sure? He has a brother in Detroit, and he's a millionaire, a Louis Kukes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION] And he has a brother in Detroit. And he-- he-- your father had a sister in Boston. And the-- the kids, the children-- two boys and a-- two sons and a daughter-- they live in New York. And you're telling me you-- he was so famous, my father, that he was known here, in United States. And, we remember your father very well. Listen to that. And everybody-- and they called right away, over there. And the two sons, my cousins, like first cousins-- tall, handsome, good-looking, professionals-- and the sister, they came over to the HIAS to see us. And they wanted to take us in downtown, and they wanted to buy us clothes, and take us in a restaurant. And I said, I don't need anything. I don't want anything. I just wanted to, somehow, to go out from this HIAS. And I'm not asking for anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- the other people-- envied me. Oy-- I came in a [NON-ENGLISH]-- how do you say, in English? A [NON-ENGLISH]-- like I came in milk and honey. I fall into such a good hands. They called right away to Detroit, to my uncle. And he said to put us on the train, and to bring us right there, to Detroit. But they-- they were afraid, because what's when-- who is he-- what is he? So anyways, he promised the-- the organization, the HIAS, the director that he's-- it's true. It's a brother. And he's going to take care of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine, they put us on the train. We came. And they said, don't be afraid-- in case something's wrong. Any time, you're welcome to come to HIAS. And he has to sign papers to the HIAS, my uncle from Detroit, that he's responsible for us. We came on the station by train. So he-- he had six children-- two sons and two daughters. He lived in Detroit, in Glendale. He took one car with a son and daughter-in-law, and with this-- and he took a daughter with a son-in-law-- with two cars, sended there. And they came over there. They took me one car, separate. They took my husband in another car, separate, from the station to their house-- to the uncle's house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They keep asking me all different kind of questions. He told them what to question me. What is your name? What is your middle-- maiden name? I say, Edith Kukes. And how was your Zayde? I said, [NON-ENGLISH], my sister, is named after the Zayde. And when were you born? What is your father's name? And from the beginning, I did-- and they asked him, too. He says, I don't know from anything that-- it's her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happens-- incidents happens, that a lot of the newcomers-- the refugees, the survivors-- when they came here and they saw rich people in America and everything, so they start to, like-- I am your family, like to give them a help. And so they thought maybe we are the same. He doesn't, because when my father died, I was nine months old. He was in America. Whenever my mother struggled to raise the children, worked like a slave all by herself, and we never heard from him-- as-- as the father's brother, as an uncle. Never help, never heard from him, anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So suddenly, bomb-- like, what am I after so many years? I survived, and suddenly I am the-- the-- his brother's child, survived Hitler? It's impossible. So I thought, maybe they're right. So fine, I told them everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came into their house. They lived on Glendale and Lynwood. And he takes me again-- the uncle-- into his house, in bedroom. And he asking me the same questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name? What is your maiden name? When you were born? When you this and that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hungry-- eight, 10 hours by train to come from New York to Detroit. And I was hungry. The baby was hungry. It was the middle of the night. I burst in tears. I say, uncle-- I didn't even said uncle. I don't know if I said uncle-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you know what? I didn't know you until now. I don't know where you are and what you are. And if you don't believe me, here. I got married. Here, I have papers, which it says my maiden name-- Edith Kukes. And it says my mother's name, my father's name. I have here the papers that my daughter was born. And they asking everything, you know, the birth certificate. Why you punishing me? Why you asking me, on the road from the-- from the train home-- and you are again asking me the same questions? Here is black and white, and this is it. So that's why he explained to me, because I didn't realize why all the questions, that maybe I just wanted-- I just wanted to-- to lean on them, that they are my family. They're wealthy people. Finally, they cooled off. They cooled off, so. God forbid, we should sleep over the same night at their house, because we are nobody, we are [NON-ENGLISH], they called us. Green, like we are. So he rented a room by a lady. She was a widow. And she-- we should going to stay there, room and board. And he paid-- he paid for this lady, until we'll find rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this lady was another lady, an elderly lady, that she rented the room. But she was an American-- you know, Jewish lady, older lady. She had children without food. And-- and the lady gave us rent and food. And they took my husband-- he had a factory, bolt and nuts, Michigan Bolt and Nuts, shipping, receiving-- a big factory. Two factories, they had. on [INAUDIBLE] then one on [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took him-- the second week, he took him to work for $0.90 an hour. And she used to cook just every single day. She used to give me one little piece of bread-- the white bread-- in the morning, and one little piece of bread in the afternoon. That's the lady, and she lived on Grand, and by Davidson, on the street, this lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the baby, she gave like not pure milk, but like blue-- 3/4 water, and 1/4 milk. And she took advantage. And every single day for dinner, she just cooked pea soup-- pea soup and pea soup. I could hardly wait that he should come home from work-- I should have a little bit of pea soup. I couldn't go out in the kitchen. I couldn't touch the light. I couldn't touch this. I was like in a jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this lady saw how she was treating-- and she goes to my uncle, and she says, she's treating us like-- like milk and honey, nothing but the best. She was a liar, you know? And she took advantage with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this other lady was there, and she saw-- when she went shopping. So she walked in to me, and she spoke to me Jewish. And she says, come on. You don't have to be-- and I was crying very hard. I say, again, the same story-- why I didn't. I-- I lived through one Hitler, now I have another Hitler. I have another ghetto, in-- in golden-- you know-- land. In America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had no business to survive. I can't take it anymore. I felt sorry for the baby-- she was constantly hungry and crying. She says, you know what? There is a A and P on Davidson. Come on with me, we'll walk to the A and P. Buy yourself a bread. So I bought a bread. She gave me the idea. I bought the white bread, and I bought one stick of butter. And she says, hide it. And when she goes away, any time you're hungry, you take a piece of bread with a piece of butter. You give it to the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can eat, so you don't have to be-- because she counted. When she left the bread in the kitchen, she counted how many slices bread left in the bread-- I shouldn't touch-- I shouldn't steal behind her back into it. I took her advice. I went to Davidson, and to the A and P supermarket. I bought a bread, and I bought a stick of butter. And I took, and my poor little [NON-ENGLISH], that I had-- the underwear and everything-- I wrapped it, and I put it in closet, in bedroom, on the shelf. And that was already in February. We came in February. It was the heating. And the heat, the furnace, was a right there. And after she left, I take the bread with butter. So the whole butter, from the water, melted all over my few clothes, the few pieces that I had, and all over the bread. And she comes, and I started to cry. I was crying like a baby. I say, Mrs. Grushkin-- her name was Mrs. Grushkin-- she was so good to me, like an angel. She said that I am her stepdaughter. She liked me like her own child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, take a look, what I did. Now I have nothing to wear. I had a nightgown. I had a slip. I had underwear and everything. And everything got melted from the-- from the butter. What can I do now? I have nothing to wear. Where should I wash? Where should I go? I'm afraid of her, of this lady, the owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, don't be afraid. I'll take care. She took the clothes to her daughter, and she washed it. And this, we throw away in garbage. And she brought it back to me, and she didn't know about it. That was-- we came in February, March, April. It's before Passover. And before Passover, it's April. We go to the uncle, and he says he's giving her more-- like he paid $50 a week-- you know, room and board and food. Now he's giving her $10 more, because Passover it costs more money to buy the food. He sat on the porch, and the children sat on the porch, and I burst in tears. And I fell on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, uncle. I say, you have no idea. I beg every day, my day, I should die. And why did I have to? I say, I survived Hitler. Now I have another Hitler. I have another concentration, another ghetto. He says, what do you mean? I say, if she world cook at least one day a potato soup, or a potato. I say, just every day, just peas-- you know the green peas-- every, single day, pea soup, and pea soup, and pea soup. Not a piece of meat, no anything. And I'm starving until-- and I told him about the bread. And I say, you know what? If you don't believe me, then you can ask Mrs. Grushkin-- the lady that lives with her. Call her up and ask her. And I tell him the story, that she took me to-- to A and P, to the market. And she takes such advantage, you [NON-ENGLISH], excuse me-- you know, you [NON-ENGLISH], and you [NON-ENGLISH], you this, and you don't know how to turn on the light, and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not even a citizen. She doesn't even know how to read and everything. She is the American. I'm the nothing. You know, because we survivors-- we're-- Anyway, so he was so upset. And I fell, almost faint. I couldn't breathe. And they were crying, too. And he said, she's such a good-for-nothing, and she tells me that you have a paradise at her house. You're not going to stay here for Passover.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on Grand and Wildemer he had two daughters, at two-family flat. One lived downstairs and one lived upstairs. He took us on the-- on the attic. OK, I had a little electric lamp, a little electric, you know, that-- a burner, with the burners. So in the bathroom, I put it in. So I went, and I went to the butcher. And I bought, already, a-- a kosher chicken. And I had, already, I bought a pat, and a bread. And I opened my eyes. First of all, I was not hungry. But I almost lost my little girl, because in the attic, the small little windows-- and she turn around, and she almost fell from the attic, all the way on the cement on this. And when I turn around, and she's almost there. So I ran fast, and I grabbed her for the skirt. And I pulled her out from the window. Another second, she would've been dead, because on the main floor outside is all sidewalk cement. So I saved her. So we were there. And then we rented a flat on-- on 12th Street. I don't know if you're familiar with 12th Street. Because that time-- at that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dexter, you know, Davidson and that places, it was like here in West Bloomfield-- very expensive. We couldn't afford it. So he made $0.90 an hour. He brought home, already, $37. I became a millionaire. I have, already, $37 for a whole week wages. So we rented the room. And I had two dresses. It was like a three-family flat. So I go in the basement Sunday to wash. I had two dresses. And there was a janitor in the basement that took care of the flat. And I go wash. I don't have the sheet, and I don't have the pillowcases, and I don't have the dress.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to cry. And I say to the janitor, you took it! And I spoke-- I didn't hardly speak yet, a broken English. You took it! You took it!

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took a big knife like that, and he comes with the knife to me, in the stomach. He says, here. I'm going to put your knife straight in the stomach, if you're going to mention one more word that I took it. Don't you dare talk to me like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got so scared, because I'm the only one in the basement and the janitor. He was a colored man, a black. So I said, no, no, no. Forget it. No, no-- in broken English, as much as I could. I said no, no, forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to my uncle and I started to cry. I said, I had two only dresses. One I washed, and one I put on. And the pillowcase and the sheet, and he took it. He went to the store and he bought the [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, that you fry-- the bacon. He bought bacon with eggs, Sunday, and he fries. And he steal my [NON-ENGLISH], and now I have, even, one dress to wear. And he started to yell at me. He says, you live in America. You don't live in Europe. You don't talk to them-- that they steal, they took it. He could have put the knife into your stomach. So-- and I say, oh, he's stealing and I should tell him thank you, that he stole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, after six-- six months, we had to move. We had to look for apartments. In three years we moved nine times from one place to another. After we moved in, in the apartment, I had to clean out, and made it everything clean and spotless. And the owner, the Jew, he came in. And he put, right away, a sign for sale. So he took more money. And we had to look again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So daytime, my daughter-- he went to work. He's at work, and I'm the one left to take care. So I go from-- and was Blaine, Pingree, and Philadelphia-- those streets and 12th Street. So my neighbor, she was very nice, in you know, in the same flat. She watched my baby, and I went to look for apartments. I walk into one apartment-- you have children? I say she was 20 months old. That was in-- in March, and she was two years in April, by the end. Anyways, so I say to him, you know what? No children. So I say, you know what? From-- you know, I used to come home, no apartment. And you have a child, no apartment. So I used to take the pillow, put on my face, and cry again-- and cry, why did I need America? Why did I have to come here? And then I walked in and I say to him, you know what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION] And I said to him, God should bless you with stones, and God should bless me with children. And I cried, to that landlord. And he was a short, little Jewish man. And I didn't spoke English. I spoke Jewish. He spoke to me Jewish. I say, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you should have stones, and God should bless me with children. And I-- so it took him by heart. And I went by the door-- I opened the door to walk away. He says, you [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], they called us [NON-ENGLISH]-- you newcomer, you come back. You come back. I have no children. Listen to that. And that's why he says, the way you said it, it took me by heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you know what? I'm sorry. You forgive me. I didn't know that you don't have any children. But how would you feel? You go every day, from apartment to apartment. She's two years old. I say, she's already four years old. I say, should I throw her away, should have throwed her, to coming here, in the ocean? What should I do? She's my child. So it hurts. It's very painful. So he says, you had-- just because you said that I should have stones, and I have no children, that's why you-- you getting the apartment. And I got the apartment. We lived there, again, for six months. And he sold it again. I made spotless, clean and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I almost got killed. I took a ladder, with the steps all the way. And I washed the step walls. It was very filthy. And he came to the [NON-ENGLISH], and he sold, again, to the black people. We was wandering. Seven times, we moved.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I want my garage, a little garage. And I want a little own, our own this. So I tried to save from a penny to a penny, $5 a week, $10 a week. And I saved up. And then I'll make a shortage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when he died, the uncle, he was a millionaire. So when he was sick, he used to say-- so then at the end, he bought us a house, a two-family. So he used to say, this is for you, the house. I don't need any home. I have plenty. I'm a millionaire-- you know, factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when he got sick he says, you wanted America? [NON-ENGLISH], work like a slave, if you wanted America. Nothing comes for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he left a will-- $2,000 for me, and $2,000 for the-- for my child. Yeah, I had already $4,000. He didn't leave him nothing-- I don't know why-- for my husband. And I took the-- and when he died, they took-- and he said, the house belongs to you. And he belonged to Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], at Beth Abraham Synagogue, was a member. And Mr. Nussbaum was the owner, that he sold the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said to us-- so when he died, the uncle died-- so the kids, right away, they put a sign on the house, for sale. So the people told us, you go ahead and fight, because he told you that the house belongs to me and not to them. But they are millionaires-- they are going-- they are lawyers. They'll take lawyers. And it was written in the-- in the will.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't fight them. I don't have any money. I don't have any lawyers. I'm not going to win. If it's not-- if it's not written in the will, I won't win.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took the $4,000 and we gave a down payment on this house. So in 1955, we moved in this house. That's it. Let's finish. But now you have three children, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my middle daughter, I didn't want to remain with one child. I thought, it's not good to remain, one child. So I want to give a name. So my older daughter is named after my father and his mother, Goldie Sara My middle daughter was born. I named her-- I named her after my mother. And so we bought-- we bought this little house. And I had those two bedrooms. And I thought I have enough. I'll go to work. And one bedroom for us, and one bedroom for the two children. And then, unexpectedly, you know, that I didn't plan it-- I figured, I have two children. I have enough. So I got pregnant with my son. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we have to pause here, now. This is the end of tape five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's make it the end, already. OK?
